title: Analogue Switch-Off Programme
summary: Led the technical delivery of a large-scale communications transformation programme preparing a geographically dispersed estate for the UK's withdrawal of analogue PSTN services through modern IP communications, improved programme intelligence and scalable operational delivery.
context: The national withdrawal of analogue PSTN services required organisations to modernise legacy telecommunications infrastructure before fixed regulatory deadlines. Across a diverse estate of more than 1,000 locations, existing telephony services, network readiness, endpoint strategy and operational processes all required assessment, redesign and coordinated delivery while maintaining continuity of business-critical communications.
challenge: Develop a scalable transformation programme capable of coordinating multiple technical workstreams, improving programme visibility, reducing uncertainty created by fragmented legacy data and delivering communications modernisation within nationally imposed timescales.
approach: Established a data-driven programme delivery model combining programme governance, analytical reporting, infrastructure planning, communications transformation and operational process design. Rather than treating each migration independently, developed reusable frameworks for planning, reporting, endpoint deployment, migration governance and decision support that could be applied consistently across the programme.
outcome: Established a repeatable programme delivery capability supporting communications modernisation across a complex national estate. Improved programme visibility, strengthened migration planning, increased confidence in delivery decisions and created scalable operational processes supporting future infrastructure transformation.

impact.whyItMattered: The programme enabled the organisation to respond proactively to a nationally mandated technology change while reducing operational risk, improving programme governance and establishing sustainable communications services for the future.
publicValue: Protected continuity of critical organisational communications
Supported long-term resilience of communications infrastructure
Improved readiness for the national withdrawal of analogue telephony
Reduced risk of service disruption across operational locations
organisationalValue: Created a repeatable transformation framework applicable across multiple sites
Improved programme governance through trusted reporting and planning
Reduced uncertainty through improved data quality and programme intelligence
Standardised delivery processes supporting future infrastructure programmes
Integrated technology, operations and business change into a single delivery model

lessons: Large-scale transformation programmes succeed by creating organisational capability rather than simply delivering technology. Trusted information, repeatable delivery processes and strong stakeholder engagement provide the foundations upon which successful technical implementation depends.

title: Programme Intelligence & Data Platform
summary: Designed and developed a programme-wide analytical data platform that transformed fragmented telecoms information into a trusted decision-support capability, enabling improved migration planning, reporting and operational governance throughout the Analogue Switch-Off programme.
context: Critical programme information existed across multiple disconnected spreadsheets, inventories and operational datasets with varying levels of quality and completeness. Without a trusted view of sites, users, devices, telephone numbers and property information, migration planning and programme reporting remained difficult and time-consuming.
challenge: Create a maintainable analytical platform capable of consolidating disparate telecoms data sources, improving data quality, supporting manual review where appropriate and establishing a trusted foundation for programme-wide reporting and operational decision making.
approach: Designed a dimensional analytical model supported by reusable Power Query transformation functions, standardised data quality rules and structured relationship mapping across telecoms assets, sites, users, devices and property reference data. Developed confidence-based matching logic, review workflows and manual override capability to balance automation with operational assurance while producing reusable reporting across the programme.
outcome: Delivered a trusted analytical platform that significantly improved data quality, reporting capability and migration planning. The resulting data model became the foundation for programme dashboards, operational reporting, migration forecasting and evidence-based decision making.

impact.whyItMattered: The platform transformed disconnected operational data into a trusted programme intelligence capability, enabling better decisions, reducing uncertainty and improving confidence in migration planning across a complex national transformation programme.
publicValue: Supported better planning for the transition away from legacy telephony services
Improved confidence in programme reporting supporting service continuity
Reduced operational risk through improved visibility of migration readiness
Enabled more informed planning for technology modernisation
organisationalValue: Established a single trusted source of programme information
Improved data quality through repeatable validation and cleansing processes
Reduced manual analysis and duplicated reporting effort
Created reusable analytical foundations supporting future reporting requirements
Improved governance through traceable data lineage and review workflows

lessons: Successful analytics programmes begin with trusted information architecture rather than visualisation. Establishing robust data models, transparent transformation logic and governed review processes created significantly greater long-term value than dashboards alone.

title: Enterprise Endpoint Deployment & Fulfilment
summary: Designed and delivered a scalable endpoint fulfilment capability supporting the organisation's transition to IP communications, introducing standardised device selection, zero-touch provisioning and operational processes for fixed and mobile endpoints.
context: The Analogue Switch-Off programme required communications endpoints to be deployed across a geographically diverse estate with differing operational requirements. Success depended not simply on selecting devices, but on designing an operational model capable of provisioning, deploying and supporting endpoints consistently at enterprise scale.
challenge: Develop a repeatable fulfilment capability capable of selecting, procuring, provisioning and deploying communication endpoints while reducing manual effort, maintaining governance and supporting multiple user personas and service requirements.
approach: Designed an end-to-end operational framework covering device standards, approval workflows, procurement, asset management, provisioning, logistics and deployment. Worked with suppliers, infrastructure teams and business stakeholders to standardise endpoint configurations and establish a largely zero-touch fulfilment process supporting both fixed and mobile communications.
outcome: Established a repeatable enterprise deployment capability that standardised endpoint delivery, reduced manual intervention, improved governance and created a scalable foundation for future communications deployments.

impact.whyItMattered: The endpoint strategy transformed device deployment from individual hardware requests into a repeatable operational capability that supported large-scale communications modernisation while improving user experience and operational efficiency.
publicValue: Supported reliable access to modern communications services
Improved consistency of user experience across geographically dispersed locations
Enabled more resilient and sustainable communications infrastructure
Reduced deployment delays for operational teams
organisationalValue: Standardised endpoint selection and lifecycle management
Reduced administrative overhead through zero-touch provisioning
Improved governance across procurement and asset management
Created a scalable fulfilment model supporting future technology programmes

lessons: Large-scale endpoint deployment is primarily an operational design challenge rather than a technical one. Standardisation, automation and clearly defined fulfilment processes are essential to delivering consistent user experiences and sustainable service operations.

title: Microsoft Teams Calling – Enterprise Communications Transformation
summary: Led the transition from traditional telephony to Microsoft Teams Calling, enabling a cloud-first communications platform that modernised enterprise voice services, simplified endpoint strategy and supported new ways of working.
context: As part of the wider Analogue Switch-Off programme, legacy telephony infrastructure required replacement with a sustainable IP-based communications platform. The project extended beyond technology implementation, requiring organisational change, new operational processes and user adoption across multiple business functions.
challenge: Deliver a large-scale migration from legacy telephony to Microsoft Teams Calling while maintaining business continuity, supporting diverse user requirements and coordinating technical, operational and organisational dependencies.
approach: Developed the migration strategy, coordinated technical delivery and worked closely with business stakeholders, infrastructure teams and external suppliers throughout the programme. Standardised deployment processes, aligned endpoint strategy with business requirements, supported user adoption through structured communications and training, and ensured legacy telephony dependencies were progressively removed in a controlled manner.
outcome: Successfully migrated more than 500 users to Microsoft Teams Calling, establishing a modern cloud-based communications platform, reducing dependence on traditional telephony infrastructure and creating a scalable foundation for future communications services.

impact.whyItMattered: The project transformed enterprise communications by replacing ageing telephony services with an integrated collaboration platform, improving organisational flexibility while supporting long-term infrastructure modernisation.
publicValue: Improved resilience of business-critical communications
Enabled more flexible and accessible ways of working
Supported continuity of services through modern communications technology
Reduced dependence on ageing telephony infrastructure
organisationalValue: Reduced operational complexity through platform consolidation
Simplified endpoint provisioning and lifecycle management
Reduced long-term infrastructure and maintenance overhead
Established a scalable communications platform supporting future digital transformation

lessons: Enterprise communications projects are organisational change programmes rather than technology deployments. Sustainable adoption depends on aligning technology, operational processes, governance and user experience throughout the transformation journey.

title: Graph-Based Visualisation of Username-Linked Data Exposure
summary: Designed and developed an ethically constrained proof-of-concept that explores how publicly available usernames can be used to infer relationships between social media platforms, data brokers and wider online identity through interactive graph visualisation.
context: Modern digital identities are distributed across hundreds of online services, many of which expose fragments of publicly accessible information. This research investigates how those fragments can be aggregated to better understand digital exposure while deliberately avoiding deterministic identification or encouraging misuse.
challenge: Design a research platform capable of visualising username-linked relationships across online services while balancing technical capability with ethical responsibility, privacy-by-design principles and misuse prevention.
approach: Designed a modular Java application with a lightweight web interface, separating platform definitions from application logic to support extensibility. Implemented confidence-based inference rather than deterministic confirmation, applied constrained output techniques to reduce misuse potential and represented inferred relationships using an interactive knowledge graph.
outcome: Produced a complete MSc dissertation supported by a functional proof-of-concept application, literature review, software architecture, ethical framework and evaluation of graph-based approaches to digital identity visualisation.

impact.whyItMattered: The project explores how publicly available information can be aggregated to reveal broader patterns of digital exposure, encouraging greater awareness of online privacy while demonstrating how technical solutions can be designed to minimise misuse.
publicValue: Promotes greater understanding of digital identity exposure
Encourages responsible awareness of online privacy risks
Demonstrates ethical approaches to OSINT research
Supports discussion around responsible software engineering and privacy-by-design
organisationalValue: Demonstrates the application of secure software engineering principles
Balances technical capability with governance and ethical considerations
Illustrates modular software architecture for future extensibility
Provides a reusable framework for visualising interconnected data relationships

lessons: Engineering capability should always be accompanied by ethical responsibility. Designing systems that are transparent, privacy-conscious and resistant to misuse is as important as solving the technical problem itself.

title: CRM Implementation
summary: Managed the implementation of a new Customer Relationship Management platform, coordinating business stakeholders, technical teams and suppliers to replace legacy systems and processes within a unified solution.
context: The organisation required a modern CRM platform capable of improving customer engagement, consolidating their fragmented processes and providing improved reporting functions across operational teams and the public.
challenge: Deliver a CRM implementation balancing technical delivery with organisational and strategic change, ensuring business requirements were captured, data migration risks were managed and users were successfully transitioned onto the new platform.
approach: Led project planning, requirements gathering, stakeholder engagement and supplier coordination throughout the project lifecycle. Ultimately shifted a failing inherited projects, into MVP focused delivery sprints by facilitating workshops, managing project governance, coordinating TDD while supported business readiness activities prior to deployment.
outcome: Successfully delivered multiple workstreams contributing into the wider CRM implementation, providing a modern customer management platform with improved business processes, reporting capability and user adoption.

impact.whyItMattered: The project supported the modernisation of local government services by reducing reliance on ageing systems and enabling more consistent, maintainable service processes.
publicValue: Improved continuity and sustainability of public-facing services
Reduced operational risk associated with legacy systems
Supported more consistent handling of citizen and service-user information
Improved staff access to consolidated service processes and records
organisationalValue: Reduced duplication across legacy systems
Simplified support and maintenance responsibilities
Improved governance around system ownership and change control
Created a stronger foundation for future reporting and service improvement

lessons: CRM projects are primarily organisational change initiatives rather than software deployments. Early stakeholder engagement, clear business ownership and structured change management were critical to successful adoption.

title: Project Management Office (PMO) Capability Framework
summary: Led an internal transformation initiative to establish a Project Management Office (PMO) capability within a growing consultancy, developing a standardised project delivery framework that captured organisational knowledge and promoted consistent delivery practices across the business.
context: As the consultancy expanded, project managers had developed individual approaches to project delivery based on extensive personal experience. While this provided flexibility, it also created inconsistencies in governance, documentation and customer delivery. The organisation recognised the need to consolidate best practice into a repeatable delivery framework without losing the practical expertise of experienced consultants.
challenge: Design a practical PMO framework capable of standardising project delivery while remaining lightweight, adaptable and valuable to experienced project managers. The solution needed to encourage adoption through collaboration rather than enforcement.
approach: Facilitated workshops with project managers across the consultancy to identify successful delivery practices, common challenges and reusable documentation. Consolidated collective knowledge into a structured project management framework incorporating governance guidance, project lifecycle stages, delivery templates, reporting standards and recommended working practices. The framework was designed to support consistency while remaining sufficiently flexible for a diverse consultancy environment.
outcome: Delivered a standardised project delivery framework that established a foundation for the consultancy's emerging PMO capability, improving consistency, supporting knowledge sharing and providing new project managers with practical guidance for successful delivery.

impact.whyItMattered: Rather than improving a single project, the initiative improved the organisation's capability to deliver every future project more consistently, efficiently and professionally.
publicValue: Indirectly improved customer outcomes through more consistent project delivery
Promoted repeatable governance and quality standards
Supported sustainable organisational growth
organisationalValue: Established the foundations of an internal Project Management Office
Captured organisational knowledge that had previously been distributed across individuals
Improved consistency of project governance and reporting
Reduced onboarding time for new project managers
Created reusable delivery templates and best practice guidance
Encouraged continuous improvement through shared learning

lessons: Effective project management frameworks are built collaboratively rather than imposed. Capturing practical experience from delivery teams produced a framework that reflected how successful projects were actually delivered, encouraging adoption through ownership rather than compliance.

title: Government Network Security & DDoS Resilience Programme
summary: Managed a high-priority consultancy project to support a government body affected by DDoS disruption, coordinating the implementation of new network security hardware and software to strengthen resilience, improve protection and support continuity of public services.
context: A government body required urgent improvement to its network security posture following DDoS-related disruption. The project involved implementing new security assets within a sensitive public-sector environment where service availability, governance, supplier coordination and risk management were critical.
challenge: Deliver security improvements at pace while maintaining operational stability, coordinating multiple technical and supplier teams, managing public-sector governance requirements and ensuring the organisation could better withstand future network-based attacks.
approach: Led project planning, stakeholder coordination, supplier management and delivery governance across the implementation lifecycle. Worked with technical specialists to coordinate deployment of network security hardware and software, manage dependencies, track risks and ensure business stakeholders remained informed throughout a high-priority security response.
outcome: Supported the successful implementation of improved network security capability, helping the government body strengthen resilience against DDoS threats, improve operational confidence and reduce exposure to future service disruption.

impact.whyItMattered: The project supported the resilience of public-sector digital services following a disruptive cyber event, helping protect availability, continuity and trust in essential government operations.
publicValue: Supported continuity of public-facing digital services
Reduced risk of disruption from future DDoS activity
Improved resilience of government network infrastructure
Helped maintain trust in critical public-sector services
organisationalValue: Strengthened network security posture
Improved resilience against denial-of-service threats
Enhanced operational confidence following a cyber disruption
Supported structured governance during a high-priority security response
Improved coordination between internal teams, suppliers and technical specialists

lessons: High-priority security projects require disciplined delivery under pressure. Successful outcomes depend on clear governance, rapid coordination, trusted technical expertise and an understanding that cyber resilience is ultimately about protecting service continuity, not simply deploying security tools.
